content.assessment: Structural delay to age 14+ directly addresses the scale problem identified by education-finance findings: Hungary's low overall spending (4.1%, rank 41/42) and instructional time (rank 32/34) mean that internal reallocation between selective and comprehensive tracks is unusually consequential (F-live-education_finance-06), and delaying selection would remove the capitation-driven funding/peer-effect loss to feeder schools altogether by eliminating the early exit point (F-live-education_finance-05). However, this is the highest-magnitude fiscal proposal among the six — it requires system-wide curriculum, teacher-training, and transition-management investment analogous to Finland's bundled reform, and the Polish 2017 case shows that poorly financed capacity transitions produce acute unplaced-student shortfalls, an affordability risk with direct cost incidence on students and municipalities.
opportunities: Reallocating the resources currently concentrated in the small 6/8-year selective segment (25,962 students) toward a unified comprehensive phase could improve system-wide value for money given Hungary's low baseline spending share (F-live-education_finance-06)
strengths: Eliminates the structural funding/peer-effect drain on feeder schools identified in F-live-education_finance-05 by removing the age-10-12 exit point entirely

Addresses the achievement-inequality mechanism (F-live-education_finance-03, cross-referenced) at a moment when Hungary's low overall education spending (F-live-education_finance-06) makes internal reallocation efficiency especially important
threats: Given already-low overall spending rank (41/42) and instructional time (32/34), a transition management failure comparable to Poland's could impose costs the system has limited fiscal slack to absorb
weaknesses: Fiscal magnitude is large and uncertain: comprehensive-phase curriculum and teacher-training bundling (per the Finnish precedent) implies substantial transition cost not itemized in the proposal
Affordability risk demonstrated by the Polish 2017 unphased transition, where capacity shortfalls imposed direct costs on displaced students and local systems
